content.problemStatement:
There are NNN people numbered from 111 to NNN such that:


**Exactly one** of these people is a thief and always lies;
All the others are honest and always tell the truth.
If the ithi^{th}ith person claims that the thief is **one** person out of Li,Li+1,Li+2,‚ãØ‚Äâ,RiL_i, L_i+1, L_i+2, \cdots, R_iLi‚Äã,Li‚Äã+1,Li‚Äã+2,‚ãØ,Ri‚Äã, determine how many people could be the thief.

It is guaranteed that at least one person can be the thief.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
First line of each test case will contain NNN, the number of people. NNN lines follow.
The ithi^{th}ith line contains two space-separated integers - LiL_iLi‚Äã and RiR_iRi‚Äã, the range of people amongst which the ithi^{th}ith person claims the thief is.


Output Format

For each test case, in the first line, output KKK, the number of possible thieves.
In the next KKK lines, output the list of people that could be the thieves, in **ascending order**.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10510^5105.
It is guaranteed that at least one person can be the thief.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Two people (numbered 111 and 222) can be thief:

Person 111 may be the thief because, the other 333 people claim that he may be the thief, whereas, he lies and claims that person 222 is the thief.
Person 222 may be the thief because, the other 333 people claim that he may be the thief, whereas, he lies and claims that person 111 is the thief.
Person 333 cannot be the thief because, he claims that the thief lies in the range [1,3][1, 3][1,3]. If he were the thief, then his statement should be false. However, since 3 lies in [1,3][1, 3][1,3], his statement would be true, which results in a contradiction. Furthermore, the first 222 people do not claim that he is the thief. Therefore, he cannot be the thief.
Person 444 cannot be the thief because the first 333 people do not claim that he is the thief.
